entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper romance and didactic miscellany preserving the 1,731-line Cambridge Northern Octavian at ff. 90rb-102vb.

holding.notes: The principal public item-level description remains the 1857 printed catalog; most CUL medieval manuscripts do not yet have modern ArchiveSearch records.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38
notes: Octavian begins after Sir Tryamour on f. 90r and Bevis of Hampton begins on f. 102v; those shared page boundaries do not make the adjacent works part of OctC.
DIMEV’s exact target locus ff. 90rb-102vb is preferred to the less precise ff. 90r-102v summaries.

physicalDescription.format: folio codex, chiefly double columns of about forty lines
notes: The 1857 catalog reports missing leaves and calls the hand uniform.
The current Database of Middle English Romance reports two scribes, with the second taking over in the middle of Octavian.
DIMEV assigns the Octavian hand LALME/eLALME LP 531, grid 445 305, Leicestershire; this is a scribal-language localization, not a secure place of manuscript production or work composition.

rights.basis: The work and manuscript witness are medieval and predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: anonymous medieval Northern Octavian text and witness readings
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editions, photography, encodings, interfaces, or supplied files.

rights.basis: CUL limits supplied copies to research, private study, or evaluation absent a separate license and requires prior written permission for reproduction and publication.
rights.component: any CUL-supplied photography, scan, or other reproduction
notes: Payment for image supply alone does not authorize public Git hosting, redistribution, publication, or print-on-demand reuse.

entities.description: The other complete manuscript witness of the Northern Octavian, at DIMEV’s ff. 98va-109rb. DIMEV v1 counts 1,628 lines and records lacunae, while the current Database of Middle English Romance manuscript page gives 1,629 lines; the disagreement is not reconciled. It is related to but not the textual source of OctC.
entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91
notes: McSparran prints its transcription on the odd EETS pages 77-179; Hudson uses it as the base manuscript for the 2006 METS text.

DIMEV v1 reports 1,628 manuscript-text lines; the current DMR manuscript page reports 1,629. Separately, the OctL electronic component contains 1,634 unique consecutive L/@N positions. That encoding metric is not used to harmonize the one-line catalog disagreement.

next_steps: Ask U-M, OTA, EETS/OUP, and relevant McSparran archival custodians for production files and accession notes.
Compare the repository text line by line with the EETS even-page transcription when lawful access is available.

next_steps: Locate explicit HTI/DLPS or project documentation for XPTR TARGORDER, FROM, and TO in this paired edition.
Compare both complete electronic components against the EETS parallel pages, preserving unmatched and many-to-one mappings.

reviewNotes: The cataloging boundary is the complete 1,731-line Cambridge witness text at ff. 90rb-102vb, not an abstract Northern version, the Lincoln witness, the Southern version, the 1986 EETS volume, the OTA package, or the physical codex as a whole.

The manuscript’s mid-fifteenth-century date and Leicestershire linguistic profile describe the witness and scribal language. They are not substituted for the work-level composition range or qualified Northeast Midlands/Yorkshire localization.

The 1857 catalog’s statement of a uniform hand conflicts with the current Database of Middle English Romance description of two scribes, the second taking over during Octavian; both claims are preserved and the exact hand boundary remains unresolved.

Rights and access statements are component-specific. The medieval text, McSparran transcription, U-M encoding, OTA package, repository copy, modern editions, printed facsimile, and any CUL-supplied photography are separate layers.
Negative public-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-15 and do not establish that no institutional, private, legacy, or unindexed surrogate exists.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item OctC, which encodes Frances McSparran’s direct working transcription of the Northern Octavian in Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, ff. 90rb-102vb. The source statement explicitly calls the text a transcription rather than the 1986 EETS edition: manuscript spelling, capitalization, and punctuation are retained, abbreviations are silently expanded, and even EETS page milestones are added only for comparison. The 1,731-line witness-specific text is distinct from the Lincoln Northern text and from the Southern Octavian. OTA item 1683 is a closely related three-file release, but its protected bitstreams were not accepted or compared, so exact package identity remains supported rather than confirmed. Complete public source objects for DIMEV v1, the 1857 CUL catalog volume, and Harriet Hudson’s 2006 edition are cached outside Git. No verified public whole-manuscript digital facsimile of Ff.2.38 was found; onsite, mediated viewing, and paid imaging routes are recorded separately from the 1979 Scolar printed facsimile.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed c. 1350; Cambridge witness copied in the middle of the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.region: England; Northeast Midlands, perhaps Yorkshire, for composition; witness scribal language separately associated with Leicestershire
catalogSummary.title: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)

catalogingSubject.label: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The witness-specific Middle English text of the Northern Octavian represented by Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, ff. 90rb-102vb, and by the repository item OctC. This cataloging subject is one complete Cambridge text of the romance, not the abstract Northern version across all witnesses, the distinct Southern version, the whole Cambridge manuscript, McSparran's working transcription, the 1986 parallel EETS edition, the OTA package containing both Cambridge and Lincoln texts, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)

locators.value: ff. 90rb-102vb; f. 90r is shared with the end of Sir Tryamour and f. 102v with the opening of Bevis of Hampton
complete Cambridge Octavian transcription, 1,731 lines; carrier and internal pagination not identified
even pp. 76-178; used by OctC only as supplied comparison pagination
octc-1683.txt; the same OTA package also contains collateral Lincoln component octl-1683.txt

notes: DIMEV supplies the column-level boundary; DMR independently corroborates the folio range and 1,731-line extent at folio-side granularity.
The XML does not encode the manuscript's scribal hand change, stanza spaces, or physical leaf boundaries.
contentParts.summary: The complete Cambridge redaction of Northern Octavian, from the opening call to listeners through the reunited family's return to Rome and final prayer.

assertion.notes: The approximate scholarly date is not converted into an invented exact normalized interval.
Witness production is distinct from the romance's earlier composition.

scope.description: Date of the Cambridge witness preserving this text

extent.description: The XML has 1,731 consecutively numbered L elements, independently matching DIMEV and the Database of Middle English Romance for the Cambridge witness. It has 18 DIV1 elements labeled as supplied line groups, 19 HEAD elements, 52 PB milestones carrying every even EETS page number from 76 through 178, 53 EPB elements, and no LG or NOTE elements. Consequently no canonical section or stanza total is asserted; the 18 divisions and 52 page breaks are representation metrics. XPTR/@TO values exhibit partial cross-version alignment behavior in an undocumented namespace and are not used to calculate extent.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse, mostly in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas with six-line stanzas also recorded by DIMEV. The repository XML preserves 1,731 lines but does not encode stanza groups.

assertion.notes: Florent's noble self-discovery, giant combat, knighting, tournaments, warfare, and courtly marriage are central rather than incidental.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English cataloging prose and supplied XML headings are editorial layers, not additional languages of the medieval work.
DIMEV's exact ff. 90rb-102vb and 1,731-line record controls the work boundary; DMR independently corroborates ff. 90r-102v and 1,731 lines.

Hudson's prose correctly identifies the Cambridge text as Northern and shorter than Lincoln, but the manuscript list on printed p. 43 accidentally labels Cambridge Southern and ends it at f. 101v. Those isolated list entries conflict with DIMEV, DMR, the XML ending, and Hudson's own discussion and are not adopted.

The 1857 CUL catalog calls the manuscript hand uniform, whereas the current DMR handlist reports two scribes with the second taking over during Octavian. Both attributed statements remain visible; the current specialist description is preferred pending direct inspection.
The XML header's 1993 date is a U-M distribution/publication-layer date, not the medieval composition date or the OTA package's 1992 issue date.
No Leicestershire place statement is assigned to the work: the DIMEV/LALME localization belongs to manuscript scribal language and may span more than one hand.

next_steps: Locate the exact hand-change line within Octavian by consulting the manuscript or complete facsimile.
Review LALME/eLALME LP 531 and scholarship assigning forms to each Ff.2.38 hand.
Keep composition, exemplar, scribal dialect, and manuscript-production place as separate claims.
openQuestions.question: Can the Northern version's Northeast Midlands, perhaps Yorkshire, composition localization be reconciled more precisely with Ff.2.38's Leicestershire-associated LALME profile and its two scribes without transferring witness language to the anonymous composer?

next_steps: Obtain an authorized complete Ff.2.38 facsimile or inspect the manuscript and 1979 Scolar facsimile.
Verify ff. 90rb-102vb against current foliation and map the second scribe's takeover line.
Document the Sir Tryamour-to-Octavian and Octavian-to-Bevis shared-surface transitions.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact current foliation, column, line, quire, and scribal-hand boundaries of the Cambridge Octavian text, including its shared opening and closing folios?

next_steps: Compare the complete OTA OctC and OctL components and the 1986 EETS parallel edition under their applicable terms.
Map the XML's zero and shifted XPTR targets without treating them as missing verse lines.
Add stanza metrics only after manuscript and editorial boundaries are explicitly crosswalked.
openQuestions.question: How do OctC L/@N values, XPTR targets, McSparran's even Cambridge pages, Lincoln cross-references, and stanza boundaries align line by line?

assertion.notes: A composition localization for the Northern version, not the known location of the Cambridge manuscript or an automatic description of both of its scribes.
Both sources qualify Yorkshire with uncertainty; it is not promoted to a certain origin.

notes: No medieval author is securely identified. Older proposed common authorship with Sir Isumbras is not treated as a personal attribution.

assertion.notes: The Northern and Southern English versions independently abbreviate and adapt an Old French Octavian; this role does not identify the Cambridge copyist.

notes: The XML source description calls this a transcription, not an edition, and distinguishes it from McSparran's published EETS edition.

scope.description: Direct working transcription of the Cambridge witness underlying the OctC encoding
1986 parallel EETS edition, consulted by OctC only for comparison pagination
Later Lincoln-based comparison edition, second edition 2006

subjects.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion

assertion.notes: Includes the false adultery accusation, separated twins, foundling and fosterage episodes, and eventual recognition.
The contrast between Florent's innate nobility and Clement's bourgeois and mercantile values is a sustained organizing theme.

summaries.value: After seven childless years, the emperor Octavian's wife bears twin sons. The emperor's mother frames the empress for adultery, and mother and children are exiled rather than burned. In the wilderness the twins are carried away by animals. The empress reaches Jerusalem with one child, the younger Octavian, while the other is sold to the Parisian burgess Clement and raised as Florent. Florent's noble instincts conflict comically with Clement's mercantile values; he defeats a Saracen giant, is recognized by his father, and loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle. When the Christian rulers are captured, the younger Octavian travels from Jerusalem with his mother and her lioness, defeats the Saracens, and reunites the family. Marsabelle converts and marries Florent, and the emperor's treacherous mother kills herself.

The Cambridge text is a shorter redaction of Northern Octavian, omitting passages retained by the Lincoln witness. Its 1,731-line direct transcription is cataloged here as a witness-specific text. The 18 supplied hundred-line XML divisions and 52 even EETS page milestones are navigation and comparison structures, not intrinsic medieval chapters or stanza counts.

assertion.notes: The qualifier prevents the 1,731-line Cambridge redaction from being mistaken for every witness of Northern Octavian.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, combining DIMEV's established version title with an explicit witness qualifier
titles.value: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)

assertion.notes: This broader title is retained as an authority form; the manifest's subject remains the Cambridge witness-specific text.

titles.value: lytyll & mykyll olde & yonge / lystenyth now to my talkynge
Jhesu lorde heuyn kynge / graunt vs all thy blessyng / and yn heuyn to abyde

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Composed c. 1350; Cambridge witness copied in the middle of the fifteenth century
work.displayTitle: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
work.preferredTitle: Octavian (Northern version, Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 text)
work.regionDisplay: England; Northeast Midlands, perhaps Yorkshire, for composition; witness scribal language separately associated with Leicestershire

subjects.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion

In the wilderness the twins are carried away by animals. The empress reaches Jerusalem with one child, the younger Octavian, while the other is sold to the Parisian burgess Clement and raised as Florent. Florent's noble instincts conflict comically with Clement's mercantile values; he defeats a Saracen giant, is recognized by his father, and loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle. When the Christian rulers are captured, the younger Octavian travels from Jerusalem with his mother and her lioness, defeats the Saracens, and reunites the family.
